01 — About

Who I Am

I'm Meet Bhojani, a finance student at Penn State's Smeal College of Business, pursuing a Bachelor of Science in Finance alongside the Smeal Real Estate Analysis & Development Certificate, with coursework spanning Security Analysis, Corporate Finance, and Real Estate Fundamentals.

What sets me apart is a dual track of experience: two summers leading data-driven marketing campaigns and retail expansion at a family-run manufacturing company in Gujarat, India, progressing from intern to Head of Marketing, while simultaneously developing the quantitative discipline of a finance analyst through DCF modeling, credit analysis, and fixed income work.

My goal is to bring both commercial instinct and analytical rigor to a role in investment banking or corporate finance, where decisions are grounded in numbers but shaped by judgment.

06 — Articles & Insights

Thought Leadership

The following articles explore current trends in finance, valuation, and business strategy — written for professionals and students navigating the markets.

Valuation5 min read

Understanding Discounted Cash Flow: A Practitioner's Primer

A practical breakdown of how DCF models work — from free cash flow projection to terminal value — and where analysts most commonly go wrong.

Every valuation debate in finance comes down to one question: what is a business actually worth? The Discounted Cash Flow model — DCF — remains the most rigorous answer available. Yet it is also one of the most frequently misapplied tools in an analyst's kit.

What a DCF Actually Measures

At its core, a DCF estimates the present value of all future cash flows a business is expected to generate. The logic is straightforward: a dollar today is worth more than a dollar tomorrow, because today's dollar can be invested. The DCF formalises this intuition through a discount rate — typically the Weighted Average Cost of Capital (WACC) — which reflects the riskiness of those future cash flows.

In practice, analysts project Free Cash Flow to the Firm (FCFF) over a five-to-ten year horizon, then calculate a Terminal Value to capture all cash flows beyond that window. The two components are discounted back to today and summed to arrive at Enterprise Value.

Where Analysts Go Wrong

The most common mistake is over-optimism in the near-term projections. Revenue growth rates that look reasonable in isolation can compound into implausible market-share assumptions over five years. The second failure point is the terminal value — which often represents 60–80% of total DCF value — making its assumptions enormously consequential. A WACC that is 1% too low, or a long-term growth rate that is 0.5% too high, can inflate intrinsic value by 20–30%.

In my Amazon equity analysis, I stress-tested three WACC scenarios and ran sensitivity tables across revenue growth assumptions for both AWS and the e-commerce segment. The discipline of that process — forcing every assumption to be explicit and defensible — is what separates a useful DCF from a number-generating exercise.

The Takeaway for Early-Career Analysts

The DCF is not a prediction machine. It is a structured conversation about the future. Used well, it forces clarity about what you believe, why you believe it, and how sensitive your conclusion is to being wrong. That intellectual discipline is what makes it indispensable — not its outputs, but its process.

Further Reading:Investopedia — DCF AnalysisDamodaran on ValuationHBR — NPV Refresher
Marketing Strategy4 min read

Data-Driven Brand Relaunches: Lessons from a Family Business

What leading an 8-episode digital campaign taught me about connecting marketing strategy directly to financial outcomes.

Most brand relaunches fail not because of bad creative work, but because marketing strategy becomes disconnected from business fundamentals. During my two summers at Shree Gita Gining and Oil Industries — a mid-size cotton ginning and oil manufacturing company in Gujarat — I learned that the most effective campaigns are the ones built on financial thinking from the start.

The Challenge: Rebuilding Market Presence

When I joined as Head of Marketing in 2025, the task was clear: the brand needed to expand from its existing retail footprint to a broader distribution network, including a partnership with Reliance Mart. This required more than advertising. It required a coordinated strategy across product design, pricing, distribution economics, and communication.

We launched an 8-episode digital campaign mapped to stages in the customer journey — awareness, consideration, and conversion — with engagement metrics tracked weekly to optimise creative decisions mid-campaign.

What the Data Revealed

The most valuable insight came from the procurement and distribution analysis I ran alongside it. By mapping production volumes against retail sellthrough rates, we identified that reinvestment decisions were being made on lagging indicators. Adjusting this led to a more responsive inventory model and directly improved the unit economics of the Reliance Mart distribution channel.

According to research published in the Harvard Business Review, companies that integrate marketing data with financial planning outperform peers by 15–20% on revenue growth.

The Strategic Lesson

Brand relaunches are not creative problems — they are allocation problems. Where do you invest limited resources for maximum return? That question requires the same analytical discipline as a capital budgeting decision. The best marketers think like investors: obsessed with return on investment, rigorous about assumptions, and honest about what the data is actually saying.

Further Reading:HBR — Marketing StrategyMcKinsey — Growth & Marketing
Corporate Finance5 min read

ROIC, Reinvestment, and the Drivers of Long-Term Equity Value

Why return on invested capital — not earnings per share — is the metric that separates companies that genuinely create value from those that only appear to.

Every quarter, financial media focuses on earnings per share. But EPS is a deeply flawed measure of business quality. It can be engineered through share buybacks, obscured by accounting choices, and inflated by debt-financed acquisitions that destroy long-term value. The metric that actually matters is Return on Invested Capital.

Why ROIC Is the Right Lens

ROIC measures how efficiently a company converts invested capital into after-tax operating profit. When ROIC exceeds the cost of capital (WACC), value is being created. When it falls below, capital is being destroyed — even if reported earnings are growing.

This distinction has enormous implications for investors. A company can report rising EPS for years while destroying shareholder value if it reinvests aggressively at returns below its cost of capital. Amazon is a textbook example of the opposite: for much of its history it reported minimal or negative earnings while building massive economic value by reinvesting at high returns in AWS infrastructure.

The Reinvestment Rate Equation

Value creation is a function of two variables: ROIC and the reinvestment rate. As Aswath Damodaran explains, sustainable growth equals ROIC multiplied by the reinvestment rate. A company with a 15% ROIC that reinvests 50% of its earnings will grow at approximately 7.5% — but only if those reinvestments earn the same 15% return.

This framework is what I applied in my Amazon DCF model. Rather than simply projecting top-line revenue, I built the model around segment-level ROIC assumptions for AWS and the e-commerce business separately.

What This Means for Analysts

Understanding ROIC changes how you read financial statements. You stop looking at the income statement in isolation and start examining the relationship between the balance sheet and operating performance. You ask: how much capital did this business consume to generate that profit? Those are the questions that separate great analysts from average ones.

Further Reading:Damodaran — Corporate FinanceMcKinsey — ValuationWSJ — Finance
Real Estate Finance5 min read · By Meet Bhojani

Why Real Estate Is a Financial Asset First — Not Just a Physical One

Most people think about real estate as location and square footage. Investors think about it as discounted cash flows, cap rates, and leverage. Here is why that distinction matters.

Growing up, I watched family members buy property and call it an investment. The word got thrown around casually — but when I started studying finance formally at Penn State, I realised that most people who own real estate don't actually think about it the way investors do. They think about it the way homeowners do. That gap is worth understanding.

The Homeowner Mindset vs. The Investor Mindset

A homeowner buys real estate for stability, pride of ownership, and the hope that prices will go up. Those are valid reasons — but they're not the same as investment logic. An investor asks a different set of questions: What income does this property generate? What are the operating expenses? And critically — given the price I'm paying today, what return does that income represent?

That return is what professionals call the capitalisation rate, or cap rate. It's simply the net operating income divided by the purchase price. A property generating $80,000 per year in net income that you buy for $1,000,000 has an 8% cap rate. Whether that's a good deal depends entirely on where interest rates are, what comparable properties yield, and what growth you expect in that income over time.

Leverage Changes Everything

The other thing that separates real estate from most financial assets is how aggressively it can be financed. You can typically purchase a commercial property with 25–35% equity and borrow the rest. When the asset appreciates and income grows, returns on equity get amplified. But leverage works both ways — in a downturn, the same amplification works against you. This is why studying debt service coverage ratios and stress-testing financing assumptions matters enormously in real estate underwriting.

What the Smeal Certificate Is Teaching Me

Pursuing the Real Estate Analysis and Development Certificate at Smeal has pushed me to think about property through a financial modelling lens — building pro formas, analysing capital stacks, and understanding how development timelines affect returns. For anyone in finance, developing fluency in real estate fundamentals opens doors that stay closed if you only speak the language of equities.

Further Reading:Investopedia — Cap RatesHBR — Real Estate
Career & Finance4 min read · By Meet Bhojani

What Two Summers in Marketing Taught Me About Being a Better Finance Analyst

Most finance students have never run a campaign, managed a vendor, or explained a strategy to someone who doesn't speak in spreadsheets. I have. Here's what crossed over.

When I tell people I spent two summers running marketing for a manufacturing company in Gujarat before pivoting hard into finance, the reaction is usually: that's an unusual path. Maybe. But those two summers gave me things that pure finance coursework couldn't — and I want to be honest about what they were.

Numbers Without Context Are Just Numbers

The first summer I was an intern. My job was to help execute digital campaigns and track their performance. What struck me immediately was how often the numbers — engagement rates, conversion percentages, cost per acquisition — were being interpreted without any real understanding of the underlying business. Someone would see a cost per acquisition drop by 15% and celebrate. I'd wonder: did we just attract cheaper customers, or better ones? That instinct — questioning what a number actually means, not just whether it went up or down — is exactly what good financial analysis requires.

Decisions Get Made With Incomplete Information

By my second summer I was leading the department. We were working on a brand relaunch and building distribution partnerships with Reliance Mart. The plans looked clear on paper. In practice, procurement timelines shifted, retailer negotiations dragged, and the data we were relying on was always a few weeks old. You had to make calls with incomplete information constantly. That is not a marketing problem — it is a universal business problem. And it is exactly what investment analysts face when building models with assumptions that will turn out to be partially wrong.

The Honest Takeaway

I'm not claiming that marketing experience makes you a better financial modeller in a technical sense. It doesn't. But it builds judgment — about businesses, about people, about the gap between a plan and its execution. And judgment, more than any individual skill, is what separates a good analyst from a great one.

Further Reading:HBR — FinanceDamodaran

Game Analytics — Win / Loss / Push Breakdown
♠ Blackjack & Finance
Blackjack is a masterclass in expected value (EV) and decision-making under uncertainty — the same analytical framework that underpins financial modelling. Basic strategy reduces the house edge below 0.5%, mirroring how a disciplined investment process minimises systematic risk. The Kelly Criterion, used by professional card counters for optimal bet sizing, is the identical formula portfolio managers apply for position sizing.
Bankroll Equity Curve
Cumulative P&L over time — mirrors a portfolio NAV chart.
Play games to see your equity curve.
Kelly Criterion — Optimal Bet Size
Based on your win rate, how much of your bankroll per bet?
Kelly f* = (bp – q) / b where b=1.

Amazon (AMZN) — Intrinsic Value Analysis
Independent Research · January 2026 · Meet Bhojani
Background & Objective

I chose Amazon for this project because it is one of the most analytically interesting companies in the market — not because it is easy to value, but because it is genuinely hard. Amazon runs two fundamentally different businesses inside one stock ticker: a high-margin, fast-growing cloud infrastructure platform (AWS) and a lower-margin, capital-intensive global retail operation. Most analysts either collapse these into a single model and lose precision, or they separate them and lose sight of how the cash flows interact. My goal was to build a model that treated each segment on its own terms, then arrive at a consolidated intrinsic value I could actually defend.

Key Assumptions
9.2%
WACC (Base Case)
18%
AWS Revenue CAGR (Yr 1–3)
3.0%
Terminal Growth Rate
11%
E-Commerce CAGR (Yr 1–3)
38%
AWS Operating Margin
5yr
Projection Horizon

The WACC of 9.2% reflects Amazon's strong investment-grade credit profile, a beta of approximately 1.15, and a risk-free rate based on the 10-year Treasury yield at the time of analysis. I ran three scenarios — bear, base, and bull — varying revenue growth and margin assumptions independently for each segment to understand how sensitive the intrinsic value was to each driver.

AWS — The Engine of Value

AWS contributed roughly 17% of Amazon's total revenue in the most recent fiscal year, yet it generates the overwhelming majority of the company's operating income — and nearly all of its economic value creation. When I modelled AWS separately, applying an 18% revenue CAGR for years one through three tapering to 12% in years four and five, and held operating margins steady at 38%, the AWS segment alone accounted for over 70% of my total intrinsic value estimate. That is not a modelling artefact — it reflects the economic reality that AWS is a high-ROIC, capital-light software business embedded inside a capital-heavy retail company.

The key risk to this segment is competitive pressure from Microsoft Azure and Google Cloud, both of which have closed the gap meaningfully over the last few years. I factored this into the bear case by reducing the AWS CAGR to 12% from year one and compressing margins by 3 percentage points — a scenario that shaved roughly 18% off my base case valuation.

E-Commerce & Advertising

Amazon's retail segment is a harder story to tell cleanly. The headline margins are thin — low single digits on product sales — but the business generates substantial free cash flow through working capital dynamics that are easy to miss if you only look at the income statement. Amazon collects cash from customers before it pays suppliers, which means the business self-funds much of its growth through the operating cycle rather than requiring external capital.

I modelled e-commerce at 11% revenue growth in the near term, reflecting continued international expansion and Prime membership penetration, with modest margin improvement as advertising revenue — which carries margins closer to 40% — grows as a proportion of the overall mix. Amazon's advertising business is now a meaningful earnings driver in its own right, and models that treat it as a footnote are underestimating future profitability.

Reinvestment & Capital Allocation

One of the most important — and underappreciated — aspects of Amazon's financial story is how aggressively the company reinvests. For most of its history, Amazon converted nearly all of its operating cash flow back into the business, whether through fulfilment centre build-outs, data centre capacity for AWS, or new business experiments. The market rewarded this with a premium valuation because ROIC on those reinvestments was demonstrably high.

In my model, I used a reinvestment rate of approximately 55% in years one through three, declining gradually as the business matures. The resulting free cash flow profile starts modest and ramps meaningfully in years four and five — which is why Amazon's valuation has always required conviction about the long-term rather than near-term earnings.

Methodology
5-Year DCF Segment Revenue Modeling WACC Sensitivity Analysis Comparable Company Analysis Precedent Transactions Margin Expansion Modeling Terminal Value (Gordon Growth) Bear / Base / Bull Scenarios

"Based on my base case assumptions, I estimated Amazon's intrinsic
